comment_text.snp4.13:2.4: If we render khema as “security” and bhūmi as “stage” then we miss the metaphor. Abhi-√pas is found at AN 3.39:11.4 and AN 5.57:15.4 where it has a similar sense of “looking forward to”.
comment_text.snp4.13:6.4: The Niddesa’s explanation of santi as “view” is not plausible. I follow Norman.
comment_text.snp4.13:7.1: Reading tapūpanissāya; see Bodhi’s note 1961. Since in prose tapojigucchāusually occur together, I treat them as such here.
comment_text.snp4.13:7.3: MN 102:11.3 shows that uddhaṁsarā is a term used of those who look to salvation “upstream” in a future life. Anutthunati rather consistently means “bemoans”, and here I think it is used in a disparaging way of those who, suffering under self-inflicted mortification, moan that purity is just around the corner.
comment_text.snp4.13:12.1: Here I follow Ñāṇadīpa, who takes the whole verse as a conditional argument, whereas Bodhi and Norman treat each couplet separately. I think the verse is making the argument that each ascetic bases their teaching on their own experience, and so if their teachings were equally valid, then purity would be attained by many (mutually exclusive) means.
comment_text.snp4.13:12.4: Cp. Dhp 165:5, where an apparently similar idea (suddhī asuddhi paccattaṁ) is Buddhist. There, the emphasis is on the personal realization of the teachings, here on different, contradictory, realizations.
comment_text.snp4.13:14.1: This signifies that we are speaking of ascetics who are, in Jayatilleke’s terms, “experientialists”, relying on their meditation experiences to justify their views. The same phrase describes the Buddha at AN 4.24:9.3.
comment_text.snp4.13:14.4: Atisitvā is normally used in the sense of someone who “overlooks” the Buddha to seek answers elsewhere. In AN 3.39:4.1 it’s used in the sense of “overlooking oneself”, which Ñāṇadīpa takes as the relevant sense here.
comment_text.snp4.13:16.3: Here the ascetic appears to refer to their meditative attainment. Compare the use of nissita at Snp 5.7.
comment_text.snp4.13:17.2: To render ñāṇa here as knowledge would be a mistake.
comment_text.snp4.13:19.2: Here, as at Snp 4.3:2.2, chanda is better read as “preference” than “desire”.

root_text.snp4.13:7.2: Atha vāpi diṭṭhaṁ va sutaṁ mutaṁ vā;
root_text.snp4.13:8.4: Sa kena vedheyya kuhiṁ va jappe”.
root_text.snp4.13:15.4: Na hi tena suddhiṁ kusalā vadanti.
root_text.snp4.13:20.2: Yaṁ kiñci diṭṭhaṁ va sutaṁ mutaṁ vā;

translation_text.snp4.13:0.2: The Longer Discourse on Arrayed for Battle
translation_text.snp4.13:1.1: “Regarding those who maintain their own view,
translation_text.snp4.13:1.2: disputing that, ‘This is the only truth’:
translation_text.snp4.13:1.3: are all of them subject only to criticism,
translation_text.snp4.13:1.4: or do some also win praise for that?”
translation_text.snp4.13:2.1: “That is a small thing, insufficient for peace,
translation_text.snp4.13:2.2: these two fruits of dispute, I say.
translation_text.snp4.13:2.3: Seeing this, one ought not dispute,
translation_text.snp4.13:2.4: looking for sanctuary in the land of no dispute.
translation_text.snp4.13:3.1: One who knows does not get involved
translation_text.snp4.13:3.2: with any of the many different conventions.
translation_text.snp4.13:3.3: Why would the uninvolved get involved,
translation_text.snp4.13:3.4: since they do not believe <j>based on the seen or the heard?
translation_text.snp4.13:4.1: Those who champion ethics <j>speak of purity through self-control;
translation_text.snp4.13:4.2: having undertaken a vow, they stick to it:
translation_text.snp4.13:4.3: ‘Let us train right here, then we will be pure.’
translation_text.snp4.13:4.4: Claiming to be skilled, they are led on to future lives.
translation_text.snp4.13:5.1: If they fall away from their precepts and vows,
translation_text.snp4.13:5.2: they tremble, having failed in their task.
translation_text.snp4.13:5.3: They pray and long for purity,
translation_text.snp4.13:5.4: like one who has lost their caravan <j>while journeying far from home.
translation_text.snp4.13:6.1: But having given up all precepts and vows,
translation_text.snp4.13:6.2: and these deeds blameworthy or blameless;
translation_text.snp4.13:7.1: Relying on mortification in disgust at sin,
translation_text.snp4.13:7.2: or else on what is seen, heard, or thought,
translation_text.snp4.13:7.3: they moan that purification <j>comes through heading upstream,
translation_text.snp4.13:7.4: not rid of craving for life after life.
translation_text.snp4.13:8.1: For one who longs there are muttered prayers,
translation_text.snp4.13:8.2: and trembling too over ideas they have formed.
translation_text.snp4.13:8.3: But one here for whom there is no passing away <j>or reappearing:
translation_text.snp4.13:8.4: why would they tremble? For what would they pray?”
translation_text.snp4.13:9.1: “The very same teaching that some say is ‘ultimate’,
translation_text.snp4.13:9.3: Which of these doctrines is true,
translation_text.snp4.13:9.4: for they all claim to be an expert?”
translation_text.snp4.13:10.1: “They say their own teaching is perfect,
translation_text.snp4.13:10.2: while the teaching of others is inferior.
translation_text.snp4.13:10.4: each saying their own conventions are the truth.
translation_text.snp4.13:11.1: If someone else’s disparagement makes you inferior,
translation_text.snp4.13:11.2: no-one in any teaching would be distinguished.
translation_text.snp4.13:11.3: For each of them says the other’s teaching is lacking,
translation_text.snp4.13:12.1: But if they honor their own teachings
translation_text.snp4.13:12.2: just as they praise their own journeys,
translation_text.snp4.13:12.3: then all doctrines would be equally valid,
translation_text.snp4.13:12.4: and purity would be specific to them.
translation_text.snp4.13:13.1: After judging among the teachings, <j>a brahmin has adopted nothing
translation_text.snp4.13:13.3: That’s why they’ve gotten over disputes,
translation_text.snp4.13:13.4: for they see no other doctrine as best.
translation_text.snp4.13:14.1: Saying, ‘I know, I see, that’s how it is’,
translation_text.snp4.13:14.2: some believe that purity comes from view.
translation_text.snp4.13:14.3: But if they’ve really seen, <j>what use is that view to them?
translation_text.snp4.13:14.4: Overlooking what matters, <j>they say purity comes from another.
translation_text.snp4.13:15.1: When a person sees, they see name and form,
translation_text.snp4.13:15.2: and having seen, they will know just these things.
translation_text.snp4.13:15.3: Gladly let them see much or little,
translation_text.snp4.13:15.4: for those who are skilled say this is no way to purity.
translation_text.snp4.13:16.1: It’s not easy to educate someone who is dogmatic,
translation_text.snp4.13:16.2: promoting a view they have formulated.
translation_text.snp4.13:16.3: Speaking of the beauty <j>in that on which they depend,
translation_text.snp4.13:16.4: they talk of purity in accord with what they saw there.
translation_text.snp4.13:17.1: The brahmin does not get involved <j>with formulating and calculating;
translation_text.snp4.13:17.2: they’re not followers of views, <j>nor kinsmen of notions.
translation_text.snp4.13:17.3: Having understood the many different conventions,
translation_text.snp4.13:17.4: they look on when others grasp.
translation_text.snp4.13:18.1: Having untied the knots here in the world,
translation_text.snp4.13:18.2: the sage takes no side among disputes.
translation_text.snp4.13:18.4: they don’t grasp when others grasp.
translation_text.snp4.13:19.1: Having given up former defilements <j>and not making new ones,
translation_text.snp4.13:19.2: not swayed by preference, <j>nor a proponent of dogma,
translation_text.snp4.13:19.3: that attentive one is released from views,
translation_text.snp4.13:19.4: not clinging to the world, <j>nor reproaching themselves.
translation_text.snp4.13:20.1: They are remote from all things
translation_text.snp4.13:20.3: With burden put down, the sage is released:
translation_text.snp4.13:20.4: not formulating, not abstaining, not longing.”

variant_text.snp4.13:8.4: kuhiṁ va jappe → kuhiṁca jappe (bj); kuhiñci jappe (sya-all, mr); kuhiñca jappe (pts-vp-pli1)
variant_text.snp4.13:12.3: Sabbeva vādā → sabbe pavādā (sya-all) | tathiyā → tathivā (si, sya-all, pts-vp-pli1)
variant_text.snp4.13:19.4: Na lippati → na limpatī (sya-all); na limpati (csp1ed)

reference_text.snp4.13:6.1: csp1ed18.419, csp2ed18.419, ms18Sn_1080, ndp20.408, pts-vp-pli176, vnp900, vns907
